15.note: Behold upon the mountains the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace — Nahum announces the fall of Nineveh as good news (the oppressor is destroyed); Isaiah 52:7 uses the same image (how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news); Paul cites Isaiah in Romans 10:15 for the gospel proclamation: the good news of peace is the gospel of Christ, and the messenger's feet are the apostles'

2.note: YHWH restoring the pride of Jacob and Israel after plunderers stripped them — the same restoration-after-exile movement that Isaiah 40:1-2 announces ('comfort my people, speak tenderly to Jerusalem, that her warfare is ended'). Nahum's oracle against Nineveh is simultaneously a comfort oracle for Israel.

The phrase 'YHWH is restoring the excellence of Jacob as he restores the excellence of Israel' points to the end-time regathering of all Israel — the same eschatological hope Paul grounds in Isaiah 59:20-21 when he declares 'all Israel will be saved.'

7.note: The decree that Nineveh is 'uncovered and carried into exile' while her slave girls moan like doves anticipates the Revelation 18 lament over Babylon, where kings and merchants weep at the desolation of the harlot city — the same judgment-by-exposure and mourning-over-fallen-empire pattern.

11.note: Nahum's taunt 'where is the den of the lions now?' exposes the lion-empire's power as illusory — its den emptied. The pattern is exactly inverted in Daniel 6: YHWH's servant is thrown into the literal lion's den of the empire, and comes out alive, while the empire's officers are destroyed by the lions. The empire's symbol becomes its judge.

13.note: 'The voice of your messengers will be heard no more' — YHWH's decree that Nineveh's ambassadors and envoys fall silent is the same motif Revelation 18:22-23 applies to Babylon: 'the voice of the bridegroom and bride will be heard in you no more.' The silencing of the empire's communications marks its total desolation.

The 'I am against you, declares the LORD of hosts' formula (Hebrew: hineni elaikh) appears repeatedly in Ezekiel's oracles against Tyre, Sidon, and Egypt. Nahum deploys it against Nineveh, establishing a prophetic tradition in which the same divine self-positioning brings down successive world empires.

1.note: 'Woe to the city of blood' (hatzir dam) — Ezekiel applies the identical phrase to Jerusalem in Ezekiel 22:2 ('will you judge the city of blood?'), showing that the condemning categories Nahum applies to Nineveh are also the categories by which covenant Israel can fall under judgment. Empire and covenant city share the same indictment structure.

Jesus' lament over Jerusalem as the city that kills prophets (Matthew 23:37-38; Luke 13:34) draws on the same woe-over-the-bloody-city tradition that Nahum launches against Nineveh. The NT transfers the Nineveh/Babylon judgment template onto the city that rejected the Messiah.

4.note: Nineveh as 'the prostitute, seductive, a mistress of sorcery, who ensnared nations with her prostitutions' — Revelation's Babylon the Great uses nearly identical language: 'with whom the kings of the earth committed fornication' and 'who made the nations drunk with the wine of her fornication' (Revelation 17:2; Nahum 18:23). Nineveh establishes the harlot-empire typology that Revelation applies to Rome and to all end-time world power.

Nineveh's sorceries that 'ensnared nations' prefigure Revelation 18:23 ('for by your sorceries all the nations were deceived'). The economic-spiritual seduction of empire is the NT Babylon's defining crime, rooted in this Nahum accusation against Nineveh.

5.note: 'I will pull your skirts over your face, expose your nakedness to the nations' — the same exposure-as-judgment imagery Ezekiel deploys against unfaithful Jerusalem in Ezekiel 16:37-39 ('I will gather all your lovers... I will expose your nakedness to them'). The prophetic tradition uses public nakedness as a metaphor for divine reversal of imperial pretension.

The beast and the ten kings 'will hate the harlot, make her desolate and naked' (Revelation 17:16) — Revelation's fulfillment of the exposure-and-humiliation pattern YHWH announces against Nineveh in this verse. The instrument of judgment turns on the empire it served.

10.note: 'Lots were cast for her nobles' — the casting of lots to divide captive populations is the same war-crime Joel 3:3 charges against the nations who scattered Israel: 'they cast lots for my people.' The nations' treatment of Israel is measured back against themselves; the lot-casting that dehumanized captives now falls on the captor.

15.note: 'Consume you like a locust swarm' — the judgment-by-locusts image Nahum applies to Nineveh's destruction becomes one of Revelation's key judgment registers: the fifth-trumpet locusts from the bottomless pit (Revelation 9:3-9) draw on Joel 2's locust army and this Nahum imagery for the eschatological assault on the unrepentant.

The locust-as-army imagery shared between Joel 2:4-5 and Nahum 3:15-17 establishes a prophetic tradition in which insect plague and military conquest are typologically fused. Revelation 9:7-9 completes the pattern.

18.note: 'Your shepherds have fallen asleep... your people are scattered on the mountains with no one to gather them' — the scattered-flock-without-a-shepherd image is the central charge in Ezekiel 34 against Israel's leaders ('my sheep were scattered because there was no shepherd'). Both point to the need for the one Good Shepherd (Ezekiel 34:23; John 10:11).

'Strike the shepherd, that the sheep may be scattered' (Zechariah 13:7), which Jesus quotes at Gethsemane (Matthew 26:31; Mark 14:27), reverses the Nahum pattern: there the enemy shepherd falls and the flock scatters in judgment; in Zechariah the true shepherd is struck, and the scattering becomes the prelude to refined remnant.

19.note: 'There is no healing for your wound; your injury is fatal' — YHWH pronounces Nineveh's wound incurable as the seal of its final judgment. Revelation 13:3 inverts the pattern: the beast has a mortal wound (as if slain) that is healed, and the whole earth marvels. The fatal-wound-of-empire that destroys Nineveh becomes the superficially-healed wound that gives the beast its blasphemous authority.

Jeremiah applies the same incurable-wound language to Israel in exile: 'there is no one to plead your cause... no healing for you' (Jeremiah 30:13). The prophet then immediately reverses it with the promise of restoration (v17). Nahum's incurable wound for Nineveh has no such reversal — the asymmetry marks the difference between covenant discipline and final judgment on the oppressor.
